name: Brand X Internet Services v. Federal Communications Commission
summary: The Court recognized the FCC's authority to interpret the Communications Act and issue regulations affecting internet service providers, subject to the constraint that interpretations must be reasonable.
significance: This case involved FCC regulatory authority over broadband internet services and the scope of the agency's power under the Communications Act, presenting similar jurisdictional questions.
legalQuestion: Whether a federal court of appeals' prior interpretation of an ambiguous statute prevents a federal agency from later adopting a different but reasonable interpretation of that same statute, and whether the FCC reasonably classified cable broadband internet service as an 'information service' rather than a 'telecommunications service' under the Communications Act.
backgroundAndFacts:
In the early 2000s, a crucial regulatory battle was brewing over how to classify broadband internet service delivered over cable lines. Under the Communications Act of 1934 (as amended by the Telecommunications Act of 1996), services fell into two categories with very different regulatory consequences. A 'telecommunications service' — essentially a service that transmits information between points chosen by the user — was subject to heavy common-carrier regulation, including the requirement that cable companies open their networks to competing internet service providers. An 'information service' — a service that offers the capability to generate, store, transform, or process information via telecommunications — faced a much lighter regulatory touch. The classification determined whether small, independent ISPs like Brand X Internet Services could demand access to cable companies' physical infrastructure to offer competing broadband service.

In 2002, the Federal Communications Commission issued a Declaratory Ruling concluding that cable broadband internet access was an 'information service' under the Act, not a 'telecommunications service.' The FCC reasoned that from the consumer's perspective, cable broadband was a single, integrated offering that provided internet functionality — email, web browsing, and other capabilities — and that consumers did not separately purchase a raw data-transmission service. Therefore, cable companies like those represented by the National Cable & Telecommunications Association (NCTA) would not be required to share their lines with competitors.

Brand X Internet Services, a small independent ISP based in California, along with other ISPs and consumer groups, challenged the FCC's ruling in the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. The Ninth Circuit sided with Brand X, striking down the FCC's classification. The Ninth Circuit reasoned that it was bound by its own prior decision in AT&T Corp. v. City of Portland (2000), in which it had concluded that cable broadband service necessarily included a telecommunications service component. Under that precedent, the court found the statute's meaning was already settled and the FCC could not adopt a conflicting interpretation.

The FCC and the NCTA petitioned the Supreme Court to review the Ninth Circuit's decision. The Supreme Court agreed to hear the case because it raised a fundamental question about the relationship between judicial interpretation and agency expertise: when a court has already construed an ambiguous federal statute, does that court's interpretation permanently lock out a different but reasonable reading by the agency Congress entrusted to administer the statute? The case also had enormous practical stakes, as the FCC's classification would shape the competitive landscape for broadband internet access across the country.

parties.party: National Cable & Telecommunications Association (NCTA) and the Federal Communications Commission
parties.coreArgument: The FCC reasonably interpreted the Communications Act when it classified cable broadband as an 'information service,' and the Ninth Circuit erred in refusing to defer to that interpretation. A prior court decision interpreting an ambiguous statute cannot permanently foreclose a federal agency from later adopting its own reasonable reading of that statute.
supportingPoints: Under the Chevron framework, when a statute administered by an agency is ambiguous, courts must defer to the agency's reasonable interpretation — and the Communications Act's definitions of 'telecommunications service' and 'information service' are genuinely ambiguous as applied to cable broadband.

From the consumer's perspective, cable broadband is a single, integrated information service that provides capabilities like email and web browsing, not a standalone telecommunications transmission that is separately offered to the public.

The Ninth Circuit's prior Portland decision was itself interpreting an ambiguous statute, so it should not be treated as a permanent construction that binds the FCC — an agency specifically charged by Congress with implementing the Communications Act.

parties.coreArgument: Cable broadband inherently involves the transmission of data between user-selected points, which is the core of a 'telecommunications service' under the statute. The Ninth Circuit correctly followed its own precedent, and the FCC cannot override settled judicial interpretations of statutory text.
supportingPoints: Cable broadband necessarily has a telecommunications component — the high-speed transmission of data — that is conceptually distinct from the information services layered on top of it, and the statute requires that this component be recognized.

Allowing agencies to overrule prior judicial interpretations of statutes would undermine the stability and predictability of the law, and would let agencies effectively rewrite statutes that courts have already construed.

Independent ISPs like Brand X depend on access to cable infrastructure to compete, and the FCC's classification would create an unregulated monopoly for cable companies over broadband internet delivery.

holding:
The Supreme Court ruled 6–3 in favor of the FCC and the NCTA, reversing the Ninth Circuit. Justice Clarence Thomas wrote the majority opinion. The Court established what has become known as the 'Brand X doctrine': when a federal court has previously interpreted an ambiguous statute, that judicial interpretation does not prevent a federal agency from later adopting a different reasonable interpretation under the Chevron deference framework. The key distinction, the Court held, is whether the prior court found the statute to be genuinely clear or merely chose the best reading of an ambiguous provision. If the statute was ambiguous, the agency — not the court — holds the interpretive authority delegated by Congress.

Applying this principle, the Court found that the Ninth Circuit's earlier Portland decision had interpreted an ambiguous statute rather than finding the statute's meaning to be unambiguous on its face. Because the statutory terms 'telecommunications service' and 'information service' did not clearly resolve whether cable broadband fell into one category or the other, the Ninth Circuit should have deferred to the FCC's interpretation rather than adhering to its own prior reading.

On the merits of the FCC's classification, the Court held that the agency's decision to treat cable broadband as a unified 'information service' was a permissible and reasonable construction of the statute. The majority accepted the FCC's reasoning that cable companies do not offer consumers a standalone telecommunications transmission as a separate product. Instead, the transmission of data is integrated into the broader internet access service. Because the statute was ambiguous on this point and the FCC's reading was reasonable, Chevron deference required upholding the agency's classification.

The decision had sweeping consequences for both administrative law and telecommunications policy. In administrative law, it clarified that agency interpretations can supersede prior judicial constructions of ambiguous statutes, giving agencies greater flexibility to adapt their regulatory approaches over time. In telecommunications policy, it freed cable broadband providers from common-carrier obligations and meant that independent ISPs like Brand X could not compel access to cable infrastructure, fundamentally shaping the competitive dynamics of the broadband market.

dissentingOpinions.author: Antonin Scalia
dissentingOpinions.coreArgument: Justice Scalia argued that the most natural reading of the Communications Act compelled the conclusion that cable broadband includes a 'telecommunications service' component, because at its core, cable broadband involves the transmission of data between points selected by the user — which is precisely what the statute defines as telecommunications. He also sharply criticized the majority's holding that agency interpretations can override prior judicial constructions, warning it would destabilize the law.
keyPoints: When a consumer uses cable broadband, the cable company is undeniably offering a transmission service — moving data from the internet to the user's computer — and the fact that information-service features are bundled together with that transmission does not erase the telecommunications component any more than a pizzeria delivering its own pizzas ceases to be a delivery service.

The majority's rule allowing agencies to overturn prior judicial interpretations turns statutory construction into a 'ping-pong game' in which the meaning of federal law changes every time an agency shifts its position, undermining the judicial role and the principle of stare decisis.

Courts, not agencies, have the constitutional duty to say what the law is, and once a court has determined the best reading of a statute, that interpretation should be treated as settled law rather than a mere placeholder awaiting agency revision.

concurringNote: Justice Stevens wrote a concurrence emphasizing that the FCC's interpretation was reasonable and that agencies are well-positioned to make complex technical classifications. Justice Breyer wrote separately to stress that Chevron deference should apply when the agency's interpretation reflects its considered, authoritative judgment following notice-and-comment procedures, rather than mere litigation positions or informal guidance.
